figures.caption: Fig. 2: Average evaluation time for solving the selected ILP classes as a function of the parameter d ∈ N. Evaluation times for the convex hull and LDT policies are omitted when their construction could not be completed within the 12-hour time budget. For the LDT policy, the dashed part of the curve indicates that the construction step returned a policy that satisfies (1.1), but without any guarantee of minimum depth.

Fig. 1: Left: Two-dimensional feasible set X = {x1, . . . ,x5} and its associated normal fan. Its cones are separated by hyperplanes normal to the faces of conv(X ) and passing through the origin. Each cone F (xi) corresponds to the set of cost vectors for which xi ∈ X is an optimal solution to Problem (P ). Note that x4 ∈ int conv(X ), so this feasible solution is never optimal for any cost vector, and its associated cone is empty. Right: LDT structure used to locate a queried cost vector within C = Rn in the normal fan F(X ) = {F (xi)}xi∈X . Gray annotations indicate candidate solutions at each internal node, and the leaf reached yields an optimal solution to Problem (P ).

Fig. 4: Flattened C representation of an LDT policy to solve any Cut(4) instance, given a cost vector c = (c12, c13, c14, c23, c24, c34) encoding the opposite of the edge weights between each pair or vertices in the graph. The total function spans over 111 lines; only the 35 first ones are displayed.
Fig. 3: Construction and evaluation time for policies based on NNS data structures. The black dashed line indicates the 12-hour time budget allowed for their construction.
Table 3: Construction statistics for different algorithmic configurations.

Table 2: LDT construction metrics. The symbol ‘–’ indicates that Algorithm 3.1 did not complete within the 12-hour time limit. When a minimum-depth LDT could not be obtained, the greedy construction depth gap is reported as ‘×’.
